answer:
Honestly, I usually prefer reading fiction over nonfiction, and I guess there are a few reasons for that. First, I think fiction lets me escape from daily life and kind of explore different worlds or perspectives that I wouldn’t experience otherwise. For example, I remember reading “The Catcher in the Rye,” and honestly, I felt so connected to Holden Caulfield even though his life is totally different from mine. It’s like the author creates an entire universe that I can get lost in, which just doesn’t happen as much with nonfiction—at least for me.

But, you know, it’s not that I dislike nonfiction. I do find biographies or books about psychology pretty intriguing sometimes. Like, I read Malcolm Gladwell’s “Outliers” a while ago, and it actually made me reflect on how opportunities can shape someone’s life. Still, if I had to choose, I’d pick fiction most of the time because I really appreciate the creativity and imagination involved. Fiction books often challenge my perspective and help me empathize with characters going through situations I’ve never faced.

Also, I think fiction enhances my vocabulary in a subtle way since authors often use more expressive language. That’s helped me, especially when I’m preparing for university in the U.S. But, I mean, with nonfiction, although the information is valuable, it sometimes feels a bit monotonous or, like, too focused on facts rather than emotions.

So overall, I definitely lean toward fiction because I enjoy stories that spark my imagination and let me see things from new angles. That’s what keeps me motivated to read regularly and explore different genres.
title: What kind of books do you prefer to read--fiction or nonfiction? Explain your reasons for your preference.
